title: Off The Hook

jokes.text: It was Tres de Cinco, or the day before Cinco de Cuatro
jokes.setup: The show has established 'Cinco de Cuatro' as a running gag — a holiday invented by Lucille Bluth to preempt Cinco de Mayo and keep Mexican workers from taking a day off
jokes.punchline: 'Tres de Cinco' is introduced as 'the day before Cinco de Cuatro,' layering a new absurdist date-name on top of the already-absurd running gag
jokes.explanation: Dense layering of established running gag with a new escalation. The logic of 'Tres de Cinco' is internally consistent with the show's absurdist calendar humor. Rewards audience familiarity.

jokes.text: They're for Cinco. In case you wanted to make confetti out of something other than the pictures that they sell of the chubby Linda Ronstadt.
jokes.setup: Buster brings Lucille 2 a gift for Cinco
jokes.punchline: The explanation reveals an extremely specific, casually cruel detail about Lucille (Bluth)'s habit of shredding photos of a 'chubby Linda Ronstadt' for confetti — implying she does this regularly and with relish
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character integration — this could only come from the Bluth family context and specifically reveals Lucille Sr.'s cruelty through Buster's breezy, loving framing. The specificity of 'chubby Linda Ronstadt' is inspired. The joke operates on multiple levels: Buster's devotion, Lucille's vanity-driven cruelty, and the absurd logistics of celebrity-photo confetti.

jokes.text: I'm with a beautiful, elegant woman! / And I think we should take it to the next level.
jokes.setup: Buster gushes about his girlfriend Lucille 2 as a beautiful, elegant woman and says he wants to take it to the next level
jokes.punchline: The narrator's deadpan delivery and the visual implication of what 'next level' means for Buster (who is emotionally still a child fixated on mother figures) makes the audience cringe at the Oedipal undertones
jokes.explanation: Works as character comedy and cringe because the audience knows Buster's pathology. Not especially original but efficiently deployed.

jokes.text: Buster had come a long way since his fear of boats was conquered… and then rekindled, only to find himself facing a bigger fear. But his biggest fear wasn't confronting a wet, loose seal; it was losing one.
jokes.setup: The narrator recaps Buster's arc with boats and seals
jokes.punchline: 'a wet, loose seal' is a double meaning — both the actual animal seal and a reference to Buster's hook hand (which replaced the arm eaten by a loose seal). 'Losing one' refers to Lucille but resonates with the seal that took his hand.
jokes.explanation: Masterful example of Arrested Development's layered wordplay. 'Wet, loose seal' works as literal animal description AND as a recurring wordplay motif on 'Lucille.' The sentence is perfectly constructed to land both meanings simultaneously. This is exactly the kind of writing that made the show legendary.

jokes.text: Wait! She doesn't have her makeup! I have her lipstick! Mother!
jokes.setup: Lucille is being taken away on the boat
jokes.punchline: Buster's most urgent concern as his mother is arrested is that she doesn't have her lipstick — revealing his complete infantilization and his mother's vanity being his primary lens
jokes.explanation: Strong character integration — Buster's priorities are hilariously wrong and completely in character. The lipstick detail is specific and earned.

jokes.text: There's about 400 witnesses that are ready to throw her underneath the boat. I don't think I can even help.
jokes.setup: Buster desperately wants Michael to help Lucille
jokes.punchline: Michael's casual, factual accounting of the impossibility of helping — '400 witnesses' delivered flatly — undercuts any hope while being darkly funny about Lucille's criminality
jokes.explanation: Solid deadpan from Michael. The specificity of '400 witnesses' is a nice touch. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Mother is going to come waltzing through that door any day now, and she is going to be screaming, 'Make sure you frisk that girl before…' [cut to silence]
jokes.setup: Buster is reassuring himself that Lucille will return and mimics what she'll say
jokes.punchline: The sentence cuts off, implying Lucille would say something scandalous or controlling about Lupe (the housekeeper), and Buster's 'I'll be fine' follows — the comedic gap between Buster's delusion and reality
jokes.explanation: The cut-off sentence and the knowing implication about Lucille's racism/classism works. Buster's cheerful mimicry of his abusive mother is darkly funny.

jokes.text: You have reduced. Well, you're not that skinny. Welcome home, chubs. Oh, you will kill me for that. And I don't want that blood on my hands.
jokes.setup: Buster is talking to a life-size photo or dummy of Lucille he has set up in the apartment
jokes.punchline: 'I don't want that blood on my hands' — a callback to Buster's hook hand, while also being a darkly literal statement about not wanting to provoke a murderous Lucille. He's practicing insults to an effigy of his mother.
jokes.explanation: The reveal that Buster is talking to a stand-in for his mother is deeply cringe-funny. 'Blood on my hands' works as dark wordplay given his hook. The escalation of Buster's behavior into full surrogate-mother territory is excellently developed here.

jokes.text: And with his mother gone, it was nice to get a chance to use the sewing machine.
jokes.setup: The narrator is summarizing how Buster has been coping without his mother
jokes.punchline: The 'silver lining' of Lucille's arrest is that Buster finally gets to use the sewing machine — implying Lucille controlled even this, and that this tiny freedom is meaningful to him
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan narrator work. The specificity of 'sewing machine' is funny — it implies Lucille gatekept even this domestic activity from her adult son. Economy is exceptional.

jokes.text: Buster talking to the Lucille dummy/stand-in: 'Buster, you always come through.' / 'Oh, Mother, that's so nice.' / 'No, no, I mean it. It's amazing work.' / 'But he knew it wasn't real. Okay, I need to stop, or I'm going to be committed.'
jokes.setup: Buster is roleplaying both sides of a conversation with his absent mother
jokes.punchline: He breaks the fourth wall of his own delusion with 'I need to stop or I'm going to be committed' — demonstrating self-awareness immediately undercut by continuing anyway
jokes.explanation: The moment of self-aware horror followed by 'At first' from the narrator — implying he continues — is excellently structured. Buster's momentary lucidity being immediately dismissed is both funny and quietly devastating.

jokes.text: And then the waiter came back, and he said, 'Oh, I'm sorry. Who are you?' And I said, 'I'm Buster Bluth. That's the fourth time I've told you.'
jokes.setup: Buster is apparently telling an anecdote to someone (possibly the Lucille stand-in)
jokes.punchline: The story reveals that Buster has been to a restaurant alone and had to repeat his name to a waiter four times — painting a picture of his complete social invisibility and irrelevance
jokes.explanation: A quietly sad and funny character beat. Buster telling this story as if it's interesting is the joke — his most memorable social outing is being forgotten four times.

jokes.text: To an uninformed observer, it looked a little like that Vince Vaughn movie Psycho.
jokes.setup: Buster has set up an elaborate surrogate-Lucille situation and is dancing with it / talking to it
jokes.punchline: The narrator's film reference conflates Vince Vaughn's Psycho remake with the original, casually misattributing the Hitchcock classic — but the more important joke is that Buster's behavior genuinely resembles Norman Bates
jokes.explanation: Brilliant layered joke. The Vince Vaughn Psycho attribution is itself a knowing joke — the 1998 remake is widely considered a failure. The narrator's casual 'to an uninformed observer' frames Buster's Norman Bates behavior as something only ignorant people would find alarming. The callback to the Bates/Bluth mother fixation is earned and thematically rich.

jokes.text: Dancing with the Stars has gone completely downhill.
jokes.setup: Buster is apparently watching TV while his mother situation deteriorates; he's been dancing with a Lucille stand-in
jokes.punchline: Buster's commentary on 'Dancing with the Stars' going downhill could refer to the actual TV show OR to his own surrogate-dancing behavior — the line works as oblivious self-commentary
jokes.explanation: A great piece of Buster obliviousness. Maximum economy — one line does double duty as literal TV critique and accidental self-assessment. The juxtaposition of his Norman Bates behavior with TV criticism is absurdly funny.

jokes.text: This pasta's way overcooked, Buster! / Well, you didn't leave me the simplest recipe, Mother.
jokes.setup: Buster is now deep in his Lucille simulation, playing both sides of a domestic argument
jokes.punchline: He's arguing with his absent mother about pasta — both the 'Lucille voice' criticizing him and his defensive response, and the detail that Lucille apparently never taught him even basic cooking
jokes.explanation: The escalation of Buster's surrogate-mother scenario into full domestic arguments lands well. 'You didn't leave me the simplest recipe' is pathetically funny — the word 'simplest' implies he couldn't handle even basic instructions.

jokes.text: Guess the Fur — the one you got after Dad had the affair with the chubby intern from Riverside.
jokes.setup: Buster and Lucille are playing a game called 'Guess the Fur' where Buster identifies Lucille's furs by feel
jokes.punchline: Each fur has a specific origin story, and this one is tied to George Sr.'s affair — Lucille apparently received this fur as compensation/apology for infidelity, and Buster knows this provenance
jokes.explanation: The premise of 'Guess the Fur' is itself perfectly Bluth — a game that exists only because Lucille has so many furs she needs a taxonomy system. The affair provenance for the fur is darkly hilarious and completely in character for both. 'Chubby intern from Riverside' echoes the 'chubby Linda Ronstadt' joke, suggesting Lucille's world is populated by her contempt for 'chubby' women.

jokes.text: Close your eyes tight and plug your ears. Just plug one ear and press the other one to the couch.
jokes.setup: Lucille is setting up the next round of Guess the Fur
jokes.punchline: Her instructions for plugging ears — 'just plug one ear and press the other to the couch' — is a casual acknowledgment of Buster's missing hand that they both treat as totally normal
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy and character integration. The instruction is delivered without any acknowledgment that it's unusual — both characters accept Buster's disability as a normal accommodation to build around. The matter-of-factness is the joke, and it's a perfect Arrested Development touch.

jokes.text: Right to the smokes, huh? Give me a minute. Let me catch my breath.
jokes.setup: Lucille has been under house arrest and immediately wants to smoke when she expresses wanting to go outside
jokes.punchline: Buster's 'give me a minute, let me catch my breath' implies he literally sprinted after her or is winded from trying to stop her, revealing the physical absurdity of monitoring his mother
jokes.explanation: Competent character beat. The image of Buster desperately chasing Lucille to the cigarettes is funny but not especially developed.

jokes.text: You've managed to combine people's fear of homosexuals and eels into one alibi.
jokes.setup: Lucille presents her legal defense strategy at a meeting that doubles as a graduation party
jokes.punchline: Buster's summary of the alibi — which somehow involves both homophobia and eels — is so specifically absurd that it becomes a perfect commentary on Bluth family chaos
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's strongest jokes. The combination of homosexuals and eels as twin fears woven into a legal alibi is inspired absurdism delivered with Buster's characteristic earnest disapproval. The economy is perfect — the joke is complete in one sentence. Highly quotable and entirely specific to this show's sensibility.

jokes.text: And he'll be wearing his Army uniform so I can pretend I'm proud of him again.
jokes.setup: Lucille is planning her court appearance and discussing Buster's role
jokes.punchline: 'So I can pretend I'm proud of him again' — Lucille's casual, explicit admission that her pride in Buster has always been performance, delivered as a logistical consideration rather than an emotional wound
jokes.explanation: Devastating and funny in equal measure. Lucille's cruelty is at its most concentrated here — not hot anger but cold logistics. 'Pretend I'm proud' is a line that could only come from this character in this family, and it lands as both a genuine joke and a portrait of abuse. Near-perfect character integration.

jokes.text: I couldn't find my Army uniform. Oh, but I thought the jury might like it if I show up as John-John Kennedy.
jokes.setup: Lucille wants Buster in his Army uniform for court; Buster has a different idea
jokes.punchline: Buster's alternative — dressing as JFK Jr. — is so specifically bizarre and contextually wrong that it becomes a perfect Buster non-sequitur, especially given the military angle
jokes.explanation: The JFK Jr. suggestion is perfectly absurd — it shows Buster's complete inability to read a room while also playing to his infantile celebrity/family fantasy. The follow-up 'that went over great last time' implies this is a repeated offense.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah, that went over great last time. And it was offensive. Half your testicle was hanging out of that thing. Good! I'm glad they saw it.
jokes.setup: Lucille dismisses Buster's JFK Jr. idea as something that went badly before
jokes.punchline: The revelation that Buster previously wore the costume and it exposed part of his anatomy — and that Buster is proud of this — escalates to unexpected body horror comedy
jokes.explanation: The escalation from costume embarrassment to anatomical exposure to Buster's defiant pride is well-structured. 'Good! I'm glad they saw it.' is a perfect Buster moment of unexpected assertiveness in the most wrong context possible.

jokes.text: Keep your ball joke to yourself, Andrew Dice Clay.
jokes.setup: Lucille is responding to the testicle discussion
jokes.punchline: Comparing Buster to Andrew Dice Clay — a famously vulgar comedian — for his unintentional anatomy exposure is a wildly disproportionate and therefore funny comparison
jokes.explanation: Lucille's pop culture reference is perfectly chosen — Andrew Dice Clay is the most aggressively un-Lucille-approved kind of comedian, making the comparison an insult and a laugh. Efficient and sharp.

jokes.text: You're a hot mess! / You're a hot mess!
jokes.setup: Lucille says she needs testimony from someone who is not a 'hot mess'; Buster objects
jokes.punchline: Both characters simultaneously call the other 'a hot mess' — confirming they are both accurately describing each other and themselves
jokes.explanation: The callback to 'hot mess' from the opening and the simultaneous mutual accusation is a nice structural payoff. Both characters are correct, which is the joke.

jokes.text: I'm going to the park!
jokes.setup: Buster and Lucille are fighting and tensions are high
jokes.punchline: Buster's declaration of independence is 'I'm going to the park' — the most childlike possible rebellion for a grown man
jokes.explanation: Three words that perfectly capture Buster's arrested development (pun intended). His version of storming out is announcing a trip to the park. Perfect economy and character integration.

jokes.text: 00 in the afternoon. / 00 a.m. somewhere. / Oh, I forgot... your mother wouldn't allow it. / I don't care what she wants. / Don't care what she wants. / I don't do everything she tells me. Let's get juiced.
jokes.setup: Lucille 2 offers Buster juice at noon; there's a 'it's 5 o'clock somewhere' exchange, then she mentions his mother
jokes.punchline: Buster's triple-protest 'I don't care what she wants / Don't care what she wants / I don't do everything she tells me' is hilariously unconvincing — the repetition reveals the opposite of what he's claiming. 'Let's get juiced' as the conclusion is also a great payoff.
jokes.explanation: The escalating denials are the joke — Buster's need to convince himself (and Lucille 2) that he's autonomous is undermined by the effort required. 'Let's get juiced' lands as a triumphant but sad declaration of fake independence.

jokes.text: Lucille 2: 'It's 2:00 in the afternoon.' / Buster: '2:00 a.m. somewhere.' — misapplying the 'it's 5:00 somewhere' logic
jokes.setup: Lucille 2 offers juice, noting the time — implying the context is drinking
jokes.punchline: Buster uses the 'it's [drinking time] somewhere' logic but substitutes 2 a.m. — an hour even worse than 5 p.m. — suggesting either profound innocence or a complete misunderstanding of the cliché
jokes.explanation: A tight wordplay joke. '2 a.m. somewhere' is a smart inversion — the substitution makes it both funnier and more Buster-appropriate (he genuinely doesn't understand the idiom OR he does and is going darker). High quotability.

jokes.text: The next morning, Buster awoke with a vicious juice hangover.
jokes.setup: Buster rebelliously decided to 'get juiced' with Lucille 2
jokes.punchline: The narrator treats a juice hangover with the same gravitas as a real hangover — the gap between the seriousness of the framing and the innocuousness of the cause (juice) is the joke
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan narrator deployment. 'Vicious juice hangover' is a great phrase — specific, absurd, and exactly right for Buster. The implication that his mother has kept him so controlled that fruit juice is his hard liquor is deeply funny.

jokes.text: Key witness! Key witness coming through. I'm a key… I… I was making two ban…
jokes.setup: Buster needs to get to his mother's trial to testify
jokes.punchline: His attempt to grandly announce himself as a 'key witness' collapses into confused stammering about making 'two ban…' (presumably bananas), and he can't even finish the sentence — he's too disoriented from the juice hangover
jokes.explanation: Tony Hale's physical comedy is implied through the stammering dialogue. The abrupt cut from 'key witness' grandeur to 'I was making two ban…' is a well-timed comedic deflation.

jokes.text: Late last night, it was drizzling rain! I'm a key witness.
jokes.setup: Buster is trying to push through a crowd to get to the trial
jokes.punchline: He suddenly shouts what appears to be a non-sequitur about last night's weather before returning to 'I'm a key witness' — the juxtaposition of weather report and legal urgency is pure disoriented Buster
jokes.explanation: The non-sequitur weather comment in the middle of Buster's legal panic is a great absurdist touch. His brain is still not working from the juice.

jokes.text: Is it over? / No, no, the grab-a-crab fest just started. / Grab-a-crab? / Oh, ma'am, um… is… is there a verdict in the 'my mother stole the Queen Mary' thing?
jokes.setup: Buster arrives at what he thinks is the trial location but is at a seafood festival
jokes.punchline: His question to a random woman — asking about the verdict in 'the my mother stole the Queen Mary thing' — reduces a major criminal trial to its most embarrassing colloquial description
jokes.explanation: 'The my mother stole the Queen Mary thing' is a fantastic line — Buster's casual reduction of his mother's major crime to a nondescript 'thing' while still including the specific detail (Queen Mary) is perfectly calibrated absurdism.

jokes.text: I can smell the cran-apple on you from here.
jokes.setup: Lucille (presumably via phone or in person) reprimands Buster for missing her trial
jokes.punchline: Lucille can identify Buster's 'cran-apple' juice bender by smell — treating it with the same disgust a parent would direct at a drunk teenager, while also being absurd because it's just cranberry-apple juice
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy and callback payoff. 'Cran-apple' is the perfect specific flavor to name — it's somehow the most juvenile possible juice, and Lucille's disgust at detecting it is both a parody of parental shame and a literal joke about juice being Buster's vice. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: I'm serving three to five. / Like the saltless pie?
jokes.setup: Lucille tells Buster she's been sentenced
jokes.punchline: Buster mishears or recontextualizes 'three to five' (years in prison) as a cooking reference — 'like the saltless pie' — implying he's heard Lucille use that phrase to describe portions/servings
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best moments. The double meaning of 'three to five' is exploited perfectly — Buster's domestic frame of reference (a 'saltless pie' with a three-to-five serving size) creates an unexpected and hilarious collision with the legal reality. The specificity of 'saltless pie' is inspired. Pure Arrested Development — maximally efficient, layered, and character-specific.

jokes.text: I hope your juicy blender bender was worth it. Don't come to visit me because I won't see you. Oh, and good luck with your night terrors. Now no one will be there to hear your screams.
jokes.setup: Lucille delivers her final words to Buster before being taken away
jokes.punchline: Her parting shots escalate from teasing ('juicy blender bender') to the genuinely cruel withdrawal of emotional support for Buster's night terrors — framed as a punishment
jokes.explanation: 'Juicy blender bender' is a great phrase. The escalation from teasing to genuinely weaponizing Buster's anxiety ('now no one will be there to hear your screams') is perfectly Lucille — the humor and the horror are inseparable.

jokes.text: Buster's response to being told no one will hear his screams: 'Won't miss it!' — defiantly, then immediately looking lost
jokes.setup: Lucille threatens to abandon Buster to his night terrors
jokes.punchline: Buster fires back 'Won't miss it!' — a defiant response implying he won't miss being the audience for his own screaming — which makes no sense and reads as pathetic bravado
jokes.explanation: The immediately-undercut defiance is classic Buster. 'Won't miss it!' doesn't even make grammatical sense as a response to the screaming threat, which adds to its pathetic charm.

jokes.text: I love to hear all about your medications and ailments and whatnot. Is that baby toe of yours still crawling over the others like a weird weed?
jokes.setup: Buster is explaining why he wants to move in with Lucille 2
jokes.punchline: His stated attractions are her 'medications and ailments' — and he demonstrates this by enthusiastically asking about her crawling baby toe, which he finds endearing
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'baby toe crawling over the others like a weird weed' is inspired — it's so particular and so clearly remembered with affection by Buster that it becomes a perfect encapsulation of his attraction to mother figures' frailties. His enthusiasm is the engine of the joke.

jokes.text: My bedroom, my bathtub with the railing…
jokes.setup: Lucille 2 is listing the things she'd share with Buster if he moved in
jokes.punchline: Her list of enticements — bedroom, bathtub 'with the railing' — includes the railing as if it's a luxury feature, a casual acknowledgment of her physical deterioration that she's presenting as a selling point to Buster
jokes.explanation: The railing detail is perfectly chosen — it's simultaneously a seduction and a geriatric accommodation, and the fact that Buster finds this appealing tells you everything. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: So, that pretend kid's bedroom, is that not an option…? / No, that room is all storage now, honey.
jokes.setup: Buster is negotiating sleeping arrangements with Lucille 2
jokes.punchline: Buster's preference is for the 'pretend kid's bedroom' — a room apparently set up to look like a child's room — and Lucille 2's casual dismissal ('all storage now') reveals this was a known option that's no longer available
jokes.explanation: Buster's desire for the literal child's bedroom is on-brand to a disturbing degree. The casualness of both parties about this preference is what makes it land.

jokes.text: Did you hear it? / Wait. Did you just drop the 2 after my name? / Oh, I hope not. That would be creepy.
jokes.setup: The narrator draws attention to whether Buster accidentally called Lucille 2 just 'Lucille'
jokes.punchline: The show itself acknowledges the significance of whether Buster said 'Lucille' (confusing the two women), and Buster's 'That would be creepy' is self-aware while being entirely oblivious — he's been treating Lucille 2 as a mother substitute the whole episode
jokes.explanation: Excellently meta moment — the show draws attention to its own naming convention while also landing as a character joke about Buster's Oedipal confusion. The narrator's 'Did you hear it?' is a rare direct audience address that works perfectly here.

jokes.text: Before you accidentally skip back too far and wind up in the middle of the Maeby episode, trust me, no '2' was spoken.
jokes.setup: The narrator is addressing the audience's potential desire to rewind and check whether Buster said 'Lucille' or 'Lucille 2'
jokes.punchline: The narrator directly addresses the streaming/DVD audience's ability to skip backward, references another character's episode, and assures us of what was said — breaking the fourth wall completely
jokes.explanation: A genuinely innovative meta-joke that acknowledges the show's Netflix/streaming format — Season 4 was Netflix-original with an anthology structure where different episodes follow different characters. The narrator talking to the audience about skipping between episodes is sui generis television comedy. The reference to 'the Maeby episode' grounds it in show continuity while acknowledging the audience's control.

jokes.text: He even pretended to be an old college flame of Lucille's to gain admittance. / Alex Trebek? / She then rephrased it in the form of a question. / Who is Alex Trebek?
jokes.setup: Buster tries a disguise to visit his mother; the guard mistakes him for someone
jokes.punchline: The guard asks 'Alex Trebek?' (implying Buster's disguise evokes the Jeopardy! host), and the narrator notes 'she then rephrased it in the form of a question' — the classic Jeopardy! format — before delivering 'Who is Alex Trebek?'
jokes.explanation: One of the finest jokes in the episode and a contender for the show's best. The setup (someone is identified as Alex Trebek) leads to the meta-narrator observation ('rephrased it in the form of a question'), which itself IS the punchline structure used to set up the actual punchline ('Who is Alex Trebek?'). The joke uses Jeopardy!'s format as both the commentary AND the delivery mechanism. Perfectly constructed, highly quotable, and completely earned.

jokes.text: Do you have a pie or a small sponge cake available?
jokes.setup: Buster has been caught in his disguise by a guard (possibly Lucille herself); the situation is tense
jokes.punchline: In an apparently embarrassing and exposed moment, Buster's immediate pivot is to ask if pie or 'a small sponge cake' is available — his priorities completely divorced from the social reality of being caught
jokes.explanation: 'A small sponge cake' is the specific detail that makes this great. Not just dessert — a small sponge cake. Buster's food requests in crisis situations are consistently hilarious.

jokes.text: I hope you are in the mood for a hot tomato. / Mmm. Soup? / With little grilled cheese sandwiches on the side. / Buster, that was innuendo. / Oh. My Lord, it's like we're speaking different languages here.
jokes.setup: Lucille 2 is attempting to be seductive with Buster
jokes.punchline: Buster genuinely thinks 'hot tomato' means tomato soup, asks about grilled cheese accompaniments, and is disappointed when corrected — followed by Lucille 2's frustrated 'it's like we're speaking different languages'
jokes.explanation: Buster's complete immunity to sexual innuendo — genuine confusion replaced by genuine hunger — is a perfect character beat. 'With little grilled cheese sandwiches on the side' extends the misunderstanding perfectly before the correction lands.

jokes.text: Is this another one of those nights where nothing is as it seems and there's no cream pie for dessert? / Holy Toledo, Buster, sometimes I think I'm nothing but a Horn & Hardart to you. / Oh! What happened to innuendo? / Let's do it quick. / It's an old restaurant. / Oh! A restaurant! / I don't care if it's old! Look who I'm dating! Let's go there. Let's go there now.
jokes.setup: Lucille 2 accuses Buster of treating her like a food service establishment (Horn & Hardart was an automat restaurant chain)
jokes.punchline: Buster doesn't know what Horn & Hardart is, assumes it's a real restaurant, and enthusiastically wants to go there immediately — completely missing the point of the accusation
jokes.explanation: Extended escalating sequence that builds beautifully. 'I don't care if it's old! Look who I'm dating!' is a perfect accidental insult that Buster means entirely as a defense of his appetite for old restaurants. The joke requires and rewards Buster's specific character — his food fixation overriding every social cue is maximally funny here.

jokes.text: Buster, you can either be a son or a lover… and right now, you're no good to me as a lover. / Maybe you're right. Maybe this has been wrong from the start. / So… Mom. / Out.
jokes.setup: Lucille 2 tells Buster he can be a son or a lover; Buster seems to accept that the romantic aspect isn't working
jokes.punchline: Buster's response to 'maybe this has been wrong from the start' is to immediately default to calling her 'Mom' — choosing son role so fast it confirms that's what he wanted all along
jokes.explanation: The punchline arrives in one word: 'Mom.' It's perfectly timed and perfectly in character — Buster doesn't hesitate, doesn't mourn the loss of the romantic relationship, just immediately slides into the mother-son dynamic. The construction is elegant and the character insight is sharp.

jokes.text: This is my Motherboy Four medal. That's when I was a kangaroo in her pouch. What'll you give me for it?
jokes.setup: Buster needs money and is trying to sell memorabilia
jokes.punchline: 'That's when I was a kangaroo in her pouch' — Buster's description of his Motherboy relationship with Lucille using the kangaroo metaphor is both accurate and horrifying, delivered completely straight
jokes.explanation: The kangaroo-in-pouch metaphor is both the most accurate possible description of the Buster-Lucille relationship and completely oblivious from Buster's perspective. It's a perfect character encapsulation delivered as a throwaway sales pitch.

jokes.text: As it turned out, Buster returned on the very day the Army policy regarding sexual orientation had changed. / Are you gay? / No. / You can tell me. / I'm not. / It's okay. / Drew, tell him. / I'm gay. / Oh. / Peter's gay. / Oh… / Sergeant Melnick's gay. / Oh. / Bi. / Oh, sorry, he's bi. / No, I'm going home for the day. / I'm gay. / Yeah, it really makes no difference to us. / We'll find something for you.
jokes.setup: Buster reenlists in the Army on the day Don't Ask Don't Tell is repealed; recruiter assumes Buster's ambiguous sexuality means he has a coming-out confession
jokes.punchline: The recruiter's insistence that Buster reveal his sexuality leads to a cascade of other soldiers coming out, one person clarifying they're 'bi,' another clarifying they're 'going home for the day' — all treated as equally important disclosures
jokes.explanation: The escalation of confessions is well-executed, and the 'No, I'm going home for the day' as a parallel disclosure to sexual orientation is the best beat. The joke treats the policy change with cheerful matter-of-factness.

jokes.text: Army recruiting scene: 'Are you gay?' / 'No.' / 'You can tell me.' / 'I'm not.' / 'It's okay.' / 'Drew, tell him.' / 'I'm gay.' / 'Peter's gay.' / 'Sergeant Melnick's gay.' / 'Bi.' / 'Sorry, he's bi.' / 'No, I'm going home for the day.' / 'I'm gay.' — all regarding the policy change
jokes.setup: Buster returns to the Army the day the sexual orientation policy changes
jokes.punchline: The Army falls over itself to demonstrate inclusivity, with everyone announcing their orientation — including one person who's just leaving for the day (misheard as 'I'm gay') and the running correction of 'bi' being upgraded to 'gay' then corrected again
jokes.explanation: The escalating list of gay soldiers is a well-structured running gag with a good mid-joke twist (the person just going home) and a genuine character correction (bi, not gay). The Army's over-eager inclusivity demonstration is both satirical and affectionate.

jokes.text: Who did Larry Kert replace in Company? / I don't know. / I know who he thought he was replacing, and it was Dean Jones. But he didn't come close.
jokes.setup: Lucille is trying to verify that the Army officer is really from the Army by asking a theater knowledge question
jokes.punchline: Lucille's 'security question' is obscure Broadway trivia, and when the officer doesn't know, she provides the answer anyway — complete with an editorial opinion ('didn't come close') — revealing her priority system
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect character joke. Lucille using Broadway knowledge as a military verification test is exactly who she is. The editorial addendum — she knows who he thought he was replacing AND disagrees with the assessment — is the character telling detail. This could only be Lucille. Exceptional character integration.

jokes.text: Mrs. Bluth, your son went down while piloting a plane over Afghanistan. / Oh, my God… / He deserves a big hand for that and we gave him one.
jokes.setup: The Army tells Lucille her son 'went down' while piloting a plane, and she's horrified
jokes.punchline: 'He deserves a big hand for that and we gave him one' — the phrase 'big hand' refers both to applause and to the literal giant bionic arm they've given Buster as compensation
jokes.explanation: A superb double-meaning joke. 'Big hand' works perfectly as both an idiomatic expression of praise and a literal description of Buster's new oversized bionic limb. The structure is immaculate — the setup implies disaster, the pivot implies praise, and the literal meaning is revealed. Economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah, that plane was in Afghanistan. / It seems the Army had taken advantage of Buster's childlike interest in games… and his childlike inability to distinguish between games and reality.
jokes.setup: The doctor says Buster went down piloting a plane over Afghanistan; Buster responds
jokes.punchline: Buster's 'that plane was in Afghanistan' reveals he was operating a drone — a video game-like interface — while sitting safely elsewhere, without realizing the planes were real
jokes.explanation: The reveal that Buster has been drone piloting is both funny and a pointed commentary on drone warfare — the gap between Buster's experience (playing games) and the real-world consequence (actual military strikes) is absurdist dark humor done well.

jokes.text: This is so much more fun than 'Guess the Fur.'
jokes.setup: Buster is playing the drone pilot game/controls with enthusiasm
jokes.punchline: He compares piloting actual military drones favorably to 'Guess the Fur' — the domestic mother game from earlier — while being completely unaware that he's conducting actual warfare
jokes.explanation: Exceptional callback and character integration. The comparison of drone warfare to a domestic parlor game is both hilarious and genuinely unsettling — exactly the tonal balance Arrested Development achieves at its best. Buster's complete equivalence of the two activities tells you everything about how the Army has exploited him. Extremely quotable and rewatch-rewarding.

jokes.text: I tell you, this kid's amazing. If I had him back in the '60s, Cuba would be the 51st state.
jokes.setup: Army officers are watching Buster pilot the drone with enthusiasm
jokes.punchline: The officer's praise — using the Bay of Pigs as a reference point — is genuinely alarming praise that simultaneously condemns Buster (a disaster of a human being is your secret weapon?) and critiques military adventurism
jokes.explanation: Solid dark humor. The historical reference grounds the absurdism in political satire. Works well as a quick beat.

jokes.text: You're not gonna get away from me, little nurse! Aah! He had been playing for 47 straight hours.
jokes.setup: Buster is deep into drone piloting
jokes.punchline: He's been playing for 47 straight hours and his target is now 'a little nurse' — the escalating inappropriateness of his drone targets, combined with the video game phrasing, underscores the horror-comedy
jokes.explanation: The 47-hour detail adds a nice escalation beat. 'Little nurse' as a target is darkly funny in context.

jokes.text: Take that, Taliban wedding!
jokes.setup: Buster is gaming enthusiastically; we've been told this is actually real drone warfare
jokes.punchline: Buster cheers targeting a Taliban wedding — the most horrifying possible thing to say cheerfully — while completely unaware of the real-world implications
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy — three words deliver a dark satirical punch about drone warfare. The joke works because of Buster's innocence; his enthusiasm makes the horror more pointed, not less. Earned vs. cheap is the weakest component — dark subject matter doesn't automatically equal good comedy, but the character framing saves it.

jokes.text: Hey, Bluth, you just took out an art museum in Madrid. / Oh, you can go to Spain in this thing?
jokes.setup: Buster has caused a drone strike on a Spanish art museum
jokes.punchline: His response to the news that he destroyed an art museum in Madrid is genuine delight that the drone can reach Spain — not guilt, not horror, but tourist enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's greatest moments. The subversion is complete and perfectly timed — the expected response is horror or apology, and Buster's actual response is vacation excitement. 'Oh, you can go to Spain in this thing?' is perfectly quotable and structurally immaculate. The character integration is near-perfect: only Buster would respond this way.

jokes.text: Maybe you should take a break. You could've hurt some innocent people. / Innocent people?! You mean I've been hurting guilty people?!
jokes.setup: The Army officer gently suggests Buster take a break for safety reasons
jokes.punchline: Buster mishears the concern about innocent people as confirmation that his previous targets were all guilty — expressing outrage that this fact was kept from him
jokes.explanation: A textbook brilliant misdirection and logical inversion. The officer's statement implies concern; Buster's parsing reverses the implication entirely. 'You mean I've been hurting guilty people?!' is a line that will always get a laugh because the logic is simultaneously wrong and internally coherent. One of the episode's best.

jokes.text: It was the first known injury to a drone pilot and the Army took it seriously. / Apparently, no one told him that they were real planes.
jokes.setup: Buster has been injured — collapsed from his chair after 47 hours
jokes.punchline: The narrator confirms that Buster genuinely didn't know the drones were real — he thought it was a video game throughout — and this is delivered as a casual aside
jokes.explanation: Confirmation of what we suspected, delivered with appropriate deadpan. Works as a punctuation to the drone sequence.

jokes.text: If this guy comes after us, they could pull funding for the whole program. So how do we buy off ol' One Arm? / Well, we do have the bionics program. / Ice cream. / Maybe we shouldn't be discussing this here.
jokes.setup: Army officers are discussing how to compensate Buster for the injury
jokes.punchline: The options on the table are: bionics (reasonable) and ice cream (Buster-specific insight into his personality). The ice cream option is delivered with the same weight as bionics.
jokes.explanation: The Army has assessed Buster correctly — ice cream is indeed a viable bribe for him. The flat delivery of ice cream as a strategic option equal to a bionic program is excellent. 'Yeah, I think we're at those kids' table' adds a nice visual gag implication.

jokes.text: So you fell out of a chair, and for this, the government gives you applause? / No… a big hand.
jokes.setup: Lucille is dismissing the severity of Buster's injury; Buster corrects her use of 'applause'
jokes.punchline: 'A big hand' — Buster corrects 'applause' with the phrase that means both applause AND the literal large bionic hand he's received, while clearly only meaning one of those things
jokes.explanation: Callback payoff to the 'big hand' joke from earlier. The double meaning lands again with even more context — now we can see the literal hand. Buster's innocent correction is the perfect delivery mechanism. High rewatch value as the payoff to the earlier setup.

jokes.text: I thought I recognized that little son of a bitch. / This is why you can't keep a job at one hospital!
jokes.setup: The doctor treating Buster apparently has a prior relationship with Lucille
jokes.punchline: Lucille recognizes the doctor and immediately shifts to attacking his career stability — suggesting she's gotten him fired from hospitals before
jokes.explanation: The implication that Lucille has driven a doctor out of multiple hospitals is perfect Lucille characterization. 'Why you can't keep a job at one hospital' implies a pattern.

jokes.text: Why does it have to be so big? / Well, they said the miniaturization comes later. / I mean, you remember how big your first cell phone was. / That was a phone. This looks like you're pointing to a place that buys your gold.
jokes.setup: Buster's new bionic arm is enormous — comically oversized
jokes.punchline: Lucille's description — 'pointing to a place that buys your gold' — is a perfect specific visual reference to the giant illuminated pointing-hand signs outside pawn/gold-buying shops
jokes.explanation: Lucille's specific pop-culture/commercial visual metaphor for the giant hand is inspired. 'A place that buys your gold' is an extremely specific and accurate image — the kind of roadside sign you instantly visualize. The economy is excellent and the specificity is what lifts this above a generic 'it's so big' joke.

jokes.text: You need to grow up, Buster, because I can't take care of you anymore. I need to 'get away-get away.' You're hopelessly hopeless, Buster. It's as if they wrote that song for my own son.
jokes.setup: Lucille is pushing Buster away, telling him to be independent
jokes.punchline: 'It's as if they wrote that song for my own son' — Lucille references a song called 'Hopelessly Hopeless' and applies it to Buster with genuine conviction, while also revealing she apparently knows a song called 'Hopelessly Hopeless' well enough to invoke it
jokes.explanation: The implication that Lucille lives her life with a rich catalog of songs perfectly describing her contempt for family members is funny. Solid character beat.

jokes.text: Once you learn how to use that thing, they're going to turn you into a killing monster. / Monster…? / This doesn't make me look sexy. / Throw it out.
jokes.setup: Lucille warns Buster the Army will turn him into a monster
jokes.punchline: Buster's concern upon hearing 'killing monster' is not fear or moral qualm — it's whether it makes him look sexy. And he decides it doesn't, and wants to throw out the arm.
jokes.explanation: Perfect Buster logic — his processing of 'killing monster' routes entirely through personal attractiveness. 'Throw it out' as the conclusion to this reasoning is absurdly logical. High character integration.

jokes.text: Well, that explains the photo shoot. / And so Buster began a rehab program he was terrified to succeed at.
jokes.setup: Context established that Buster wanted to look sexy and was worried about looking like a monster
jokes.punchline: The narrator references a 'photo shoot' (implying Buster did one with the bionic arm, presumably trying to look attractive) and notes he was 'terrified to succeed' at rehab — meaning succeeding would mean leaving the Army's comfortable care
jokes.explanation: 'Terrified to succeed at' is a great phrase for Buster's psychology. The photo shoot reference is a funny implied visual we don't see.

jokes.text: Bionic arm rehab sequence — 'Too hard.' / 'Oh, I got some on you.' / 'Yeah, you did get some on me.' / 'That's on her.' / 'It was too hard, as well.'
jokes.setup: Buster is attempting to use his new bionic arm in rehab — opening a cabinet, grabbing toothpaste
jokes.punchline: The arm is clearly too powerful — he 'gets some on' the rehab worker (presumably toothpaste or similar) — and the politeness with which everyone discusses this is the joke
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy implied through dialogue. The sequence works as escalation but the dialogue-only transcript limits how much comedic punch it carries. The politeness of 'that's on her' as a judgment about responsibility is a nice touch.

jokes.text: Physical therapy: Buster uses new hand, immediately gets 'toothpaste on her' — therapist repeats 'too hard... too hard' multiple times in escalating awkwardness
jokes.setup: Buster attempts basic tasks with the bionic hand
jokes.punchline: He consistently applies too much force, getting various substances on the therapist — the escalating 'too hard' refrain becomes its own cringe comedy rhythm
jokes.explanation: The slapstick works in visual terms but the transcript captures it imperfectly. 'Too hard' as a repeated beat is functional but not especially crafted. Middle-of-the-road physical comedy.

jokes.text: Let's go ahead and fast-forward through the commercials. / Okay, that's too fa… okay, stop. / Well, there's Jon Cryer. / Stop! That's Jon Cryer! / Well, look at that… we're back at the Progressive Insurance lady. / I'm better off just watching the commercials!
jokes.setup: Buster is trying to use his bionic arm to control a TV remote
jokes.punchline: The arm is too powerful/fast to control — it keeps going too far and landing on Jon Cryer and the Progressive Insurance spokesperson — and Buster concludes he's better off watching commercials
jokes.explanation: Jon Cryer is the specific celebrity detail that makes this work — Two and a Half Men was a then-contemporary ratings behemoth of lowbrow comedy, making it a perfect target for an AD joke. The escalation from 'too fast' to 'might as well watch commercials' is well-structured.

jokes.text: Hope this guy likes the music acts on Saturday Night Live because he's gonna be watching a lot of them. / How many remotes do we have? / That was the only one. / There's seven monitors here… we have one remote.
jokes.setup: Army observers watching Buster's rehab realize he's broken the only remote
jokes.punchline: The Army has seven monitors and one remote, and the remote is now controlled by a man with an unpredictable bionic arm — the logistics of this situation are the joke
jokes.explanation: Competent situational comedy. The SNL music acts detail is a nice specific insult (they were frequently criticized). The logistical absurdity of one remote for seven monitors is a solid observational beat.

jokes.text: Do you have any ice cream? / Maybe it would work with ice cream. / And now he wants ice cream. / He's using us.
jokes.setup: Buster has failed at yet another rehab task and pivots to requesting ice cream
jokes.punchline: The Army staff's realization that 'he's using us' — that Buster has figured out the ice cream option discussed earlier and is deliberately failing to stay comfortable — is a moment of genuine insight that makes Buster unexpectedly shrewd
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback payoff. The revelation that Buster — ostensibly naive and childlike — has been deliberately gaming the system with ice cream requests is a funny inversion. 'He's using us' lands as a genuine surprise. The callback to the Army officers' earlier strategy discussion makes this work.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, we're running behind with your testing, Buster. We'll just be a few more minutes. / Oh… well…! / And it was then that Gunner learned why it was called the Q-test. / Aren't you the 'cu-test'?
jokes.setup: Buster is told he has to take the 'Q-test'; the narrator foreshadows the name's significance
jokes.punchline: The Q-test is named because Buster's response to being left alone is to say 'Aren't you the cu-test?' — the Q-test measures how cute-obsessed or easily distracted by flattery he is
jokes.explanation: Excellent wordplay construction. The narrator's buildup ('it was then that Gunner learned why it was called the Q-test') creates the perfect setup for the phonetic pun. 'Cu-test' = 'Q-test' — and it reveals Buster's vulnerability to flattery as an Army-documentable trait. Efficient and clever.

jokes.text: The Q-test: Buster sees an attractive female therapist through a two-way mirror and the test name is revealed — 'Aren't you the cu-test?'
jokes.setup: The Army administers the 'Q-test' to assess Buster's readiness
jokes.punchline: Buster's reaction to seeing the attractive therapist through the mirror is to say 'Oh... Aren't you the cu-test?' — the Q-test name is a pun on 'cutest'
jokes.explanation: The Q-test/cu-test pun is classic Arrested Development — the joke is planted in the test name and paid off in Buster's natural response. Good structure with the pun embedded in the institutional framework.

jokes.text: Now, he can't see us at all… that's correct? / I know! / Why wouldn't he be able to see us? / Because it's a mirror. / No, it's a window. / Oh, hi.
jokes.setup: The Army observers are watching Buster through what they believe is a one-way mirror
jokes.punchline: The staff disagree about whether it's a mirror or a window — and Buster waves hi at them, confirming it's a regular window and they've been visible to him all along
jokes.explanation: Classic comedic construction — the audience and characters discover simultaneously that the observation is mutual. 'Oh, hi' from Buster is the perfect payoff word.

jokes.text: Buster was being discharged from the Army. / So where am I supposed to go? What am I supposed to do? I have nowhere to live. / You should've thought about that before you let that cat live.
jokes.setup: Buster is being discharged and asks the classic lost-person questions
jokes.punchline: 'You should've thought about that before you let that cat live' — a completely unexpected rationale for his discharge, implying Buster spared a cat when he should have done otherwise, and that this is the reason for everything
jokes.explanation: The cat detail is perfectly absurd — we have no context for what this cat is or why it matters, which makes it funnier. The delivery implies it's an established fact being referenced, giving it the weight of a known incident.

jokes.text: You're a veteran and you have nowhere to go? / Oh, it's not Army's fault. / I'm a monster. / And what makes you think you're a monster? / What do you see when you look at this? / I see a veteran whose hand I'd like to shake. / Not that one.
jokes.setup: Ophelia Love meets Buster and offers compassion; he identifies himself as a monster and gestures to his bionic hand
jokes.punchline: Ophelia says she'd like to shake his hand — Buster says 'not that one' — a physical comedy beat implying she reached for the bionic arm and he redirects to the normal hand (or the hook stub)
jokes.explanation: Three-word punchline with perfect economy. 'Not that one' is exactly right — it sets up a physical comedy moment with a minimum of words and maximum of implication. High quotability for such a brief line.

jokes.text: Perfect! Wait, you didn't say 'nothing,' did you? / Something. / 'Something.' Got it.
jokes.setup: Ophelia says they 'may have something' for Buster
jokes.punchline: Buster immediately checks that she didn't say 'nothing' — his fear of being dismissed is so ingrained he needs verbal confirmation that a positive offer was made — and gets reassurance from her clarification
jokes.explanation: A callback to the Army recruitment beat where Buster needed the same verbal confirmation. His anxiety about positive responses is consistently funny. Good character integration.

jokes.text: It's like that movie The Blind Side. / I suppose we could use a Blind Side Monster. / Well, we shouldn't call him a monster. / That's why I love you, Ophelia. You've got a heart as big as that Blind Side Monster's hand.
jokes.setup: Ophelia compares taking in Buster to The Blind Side; Herbert agrees but uses 'monster' label
jokes.punchline: Herbert's correction-then-compliment — 'That's why I love you… you've got a heart as big as that Blind Side Monster's hand' — immediately reintroduces the 'monster' label he just agreed to drop, in a supposed compliment
jokes.explanation: Herbert's inadvertent reintroduction of 'monster' as a term of endearment in a compliment is a perfect character beat. His 'heart as big as that Blind Side Monster's hand' is logically tortured and funny. Establishes Herbert's oblivious condescension efficiently.

jokes.text: This table is for National Honor Society members only. / Really. / Well, what about these two guys? / Were they invited? / Light drizzle… and thunder. / What?! / See, light drizzle will just annoy you, but see, thunder… will 'F' you up!
jokes.setup: Buster confronts bullies who are blocking an NHS table; he threatens them with weather metaphors
jokes.punchline: 'Light drizzle will just annoy you, but thunder will F you up' — Buster's threat system is based entirely on weather classification, which is menacing in his delivery while being absurdly innocent in its content
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke — Buster's meteorological threat system is perfectly absurd and character-specific. He's the person who would use weather as a fighting metaphor, delivered completely earnestly. 'Thunder will F you up' is highly quotable. The build from 'light drizzle' to 'thunder' follows proper threat-escalation logic, which makes the absurdism more satisfying.

jokes.text: Buster even received a badge from an undercover cop that was posing as a teacher. / You're never too old to stand up to a bully. Whether he's an attention hog who looks just like you, or whether he's half your size.
jokes.setup: An undercover cop gives Buster a badge for stopping the bullies
jokes.punchline: The cop's speech about bullies — 'whether he's an attention hog who looks just like you, or whether he's half your size' — describes both the bullies AND the dynamic of Lucille bullying Buster, and is delivered as generic inspiration
jokes.explanation: The cop's generic anti-bullying speech inadvertently also describes the Lucille-Buster dynamic. Solid ironic double-meaning.

jokes.text: Pour in the liquor… Don't want the boys to see… You throw in some cola… Got a Coke and Hennessy! Just for you, Mother Love.
jokes.setup: Buster is making a drink for Ophelia, singing a modified version of his earlier recipe song
jokes.punchline: It's a direct callback to the vodka martini song from the beginning — same structure, now applied to a different mother figure (Ophelia = 'Mother Love'). He's transferred his mother-servitude completely.
jokes.explanation: Effective structural callback — the same song, now for Ophelia, confirms the transfer of mother-fixation. 'Mother Love' as the triumphant final lyric mirrors the original 'Mother.' The callback rewards attentive viewers and crystallizes Buster's character arc (or lack thereof).

jokes.text: But your other hand is waving me on. / Oh, is that going again? / I think it's on demo. / Do you have a pencil or a paper clip?
jokes.setup: Buster tells Ophelia to stop complimenting him but his bionic arm is independently waving her on
jokes.punchline: Buster's bionic arm has a 'demo mode' that waves people toward him independently of his conscious control, and he needs a pencil or paper clip to reset it — treating his arm like a malfunctioning device
jokes.explanation: The 'demo mode' concept for a bionic arm is inspired — it treats a military prosthetic like a consumer electronic. The paper clip request is a perfect mundane solution to an absurd problem.

jokes.text: Let me show you what a government handout looks like. / Hold your government hand out. / Yeah! And let's give freedom from government a big thumbs-up!
jokes.setup: Herbert Love is using Buster at a campaign event to demonstrate anti-government-handout sentiment
jokes.punchline: Herbert uses Buster's bionic (government-provided) arm as a prop in his anti-government-handout speech — while Buster gives a thumbs-up with the very arm that IS a government handout. The irony is complete.
jokes.explanation: A genuinely excellent political satire joke embedded in character comedy. Herbert using a veteran's government-provided bionic arm to campaign against government handouts is structurally perfect irony — and 'government hand out' as both pun (handout/hand out) and policy position is excellent wordplay. One of the episode's best constructed jokes.

jokes.text: It's like I said about contraception: If you want me to pay for her diaphragm, I get to put my hand in her… / Shoe! / Nice stop, Blind Side Monster. / Buster… Blind Side Buster.
jokes.setup: Herbert is making an inappropriate campaign statement about contraception
jokes.punchline: Buster's bionic arm shoots out and covers Herbert's mouth (implied) before he can finish the sentence — replacing what was clearly a vulgar ending with 'Shoe!' Herbert's grateful 'Nice stop' and the name correction ('Buster') are the capper
jokes.explanation: A beautifully set up physical-comedy-via-dialogue joke. The audience fills in the missing word, making the censorship itself the punchline. Buster accidentally becoming a PR savior through bionic arm misfires is wonderfully absurd. 'Shoe!' as the stand-in word is perfect. The running gag of his name evolving toward 'Blind Side Buster' is a nice structural addition.

jokes.text: Give him a big hand back! [Buster's bionic arm does something dramatic]
jokes.setup: The crowd cheers for Buster at the campaign event
jokes.punchline: 'Give him a big hand' — the recurring double-meaning phrase (applause + literal bionic hand) lands again in a crowd-cheer context
jokes.explanation: Third deployment of the 'big hand' pun. Each usage is in a different context — the joke rewards those tracking it. By the third time, it's also a running gag, and the cumulative effect adds impact.

jokes.text: You've been flirting with me since day one. What? Bringing me these flowers, offering to undress me. What about the singing? 'Sealed with a kiss' through the door while I bathed? / Right. / Mother stuff. / I mean, you're married.
jokes.setup: Ophelia accuses Buster of flirting; he denies it by reframing each romantic gesture as 'mother stuff'
jokes.punchline: 'Mother stuff' as a category that includes serenading someone through a bathroom door while they bathe — delivered completely earnestly by Buster
jokes.explanation: 'Mother stuff' as a genre of behavior that includes bathroom-door serenading is a perfect Buster encapsulation. The list of actions (flowers, undressing, singing through bathroom door) is an escalating reveal of how inappropriate his 'mother stuff' category is.

jokes.text: Buster: 'Right. Mother stuff.' — dismissing all his romantic behavior as maternal-bonding activities
jokes.setup: Ophelia lists everything Buster did that she interpreted as flirting
jokes.punchline: 'Mother stuff' — Buster categorizes the entire list of romantic gestures under the umbrella term 'mother stuff,' which implies there's a whole taxonomy in his head of what constitutes mother activities, and all of Ophelia's list qualifies
jokes.explanation: 'Mother stuff' is an exceptional two-word joke. The completeness of Buster's categorization — flowers, undressing, singing outside the bathroom = 'Mother stuff' — is both perfectly in character and a devastating portrait of his arrested development. Economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: That is my husband, with a prostitute. / I've worn a captain's hat with someone who had hair like that.
jokes.setup: Ophelia shows Buster a photo of Herbert with a prostitute
jokes.punchline: Buster's response to seeing the prostitute photo is to identify with the prostitute's hairstyle via his captain's hat experience — missing entirely the scandal of the situation
jokes.explanation: Buster's complete misdirection from the scandal to hair recognition is classic character comedy. 'I've worn a captain's hat with someone who had hair like that' implies a very specific shared experience with someone with that hairstyle, which raises further questions. The obliviousness is perfect.

jokes.text: You can either be a son or a lover to me. / And I already have two sons.
jokes.setup: This mirrors Lucille 2's earlier line to Buster ('son or lover')
jokes.punchline: The callback to the Lucille 2 line — here from Ophelia Love — shows Buster is caught in the same dynamic with a new woman. The additional 'I already have two sons' adds the practical rejection on top of the thematic one.
jokes.explanation: Excellent structural callback — the exact same phrasing, now from Ophelia, confirms Buster is trapped in a repeating pattern with mother figures. 'I already have two sons' is the kicker that makes it land as rejection rather than upgrade. The repetition is thematically meaningful AND comedically satisfying.

jokes.text: I came here to thank you. You were trying to push me out of the nest… and you were right. / Really? / Yes. I've grown. And I'm in love. / Hamster or turtle? / No, Mother. Hamsters are gross. / It's a female. / Human. Female human. Whole thing.
jokes.setup: Buster announces he's in love; Lucille assumes it's a pet
jokes.punchline: 'Human. Female human. Whole thing.' — Buster's escalating clarifications reveal how low Lucille's expectations of him are, and the need to specify 'whole thing' is the perfect final beat
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest comedic sequences. The escalation structure is impeccable — each clarification reveals something more damning about what Lucille expected from Buster. 'Human. Female human. Whole thing.' is a perfect comedic triple, each word carrying more weight. 'Whole thing' implies Lucille might have accepted a partial person. 'Hamsters are gross' is an inspired character detail. Extremely quotable and highly rewatch-rewarding.

jokes.text: So your parental responsibilities are complete. Hot. / I no longer need you. / Well, you don't have to go that far. / I'm not a mother boy anymore. I'm a mother man.
jokes.setup: Buster declares his independence from Lucille and from the Motherboy dynamic
jokes.punchline: 'I'm not a mother boy anymore. I'm a mother man.' — Buster's declaration of maturity is undermined by the fact that he's still defining himself entirely in terms of his relationship to mothers, just now as a 'man' version
jokes.explanation: The episode's thematic punchline and one of its greatest jokes. 'I'm a mother man' perfectly encapsulates Buster's complete lack of self-awareness — his declaration of growth still entirely revolves around the mother fixation he thinks he's transcending. The rhythm of 'not a mother boy / a mother man' is perfect, and the comedic irony is total. Lucille's 'Hot' as an interjection and 'you don't have to go that far' round the scene perfectly.

jokes.text: And do you want to hear something so surprising? She started out as my mother figure. / Not surprising.
jokes.setup: Buster reveals to Michael that his love interest started as a mother figure
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'not surprising' — complete deadpan — speaks for the audience and for everyone who has watched Buster's entire life
jokes.explanation: Perfect two-word reaction that does more work than a paragraph. Michael speaking for the audience is a classic Arrested Development move — his deadpan functions as the straight-man puncture of Buster's obliviousness.

jokes.text: After I've zipped down, you can't expect me to zip up! / I know that. / But you have to leave.
jokes.setup: Buster is told he has to leave Ophelia's house after she reconciles with Herbert
jokes.punchline: 'After I've zipped down, you can't expect me to zip up!' — an apparent clothing/intimacy metaphor that also works as an almost-euphemism for his emotional state. Buster means it literally (a zipper?) but it functions as a statement about emotional openness.
jokes.explanation: The zipper metaphor works simultaneously as literal (clothing) and emotional (vulnerability), which is very Arrested Development. Buster's earnest delivery makes the double meaning funnier.

jokes.text: Oh, no, I'm okay with the mothering. I mean, he had you first, I'm here to stay. / It's just a little mother/son bump in the road. / Mother, I ain't going nowhere. / Your hand is broken again. / No, it's just my heart. / Oh, it's my hand, too, actually.
jokes.setup: Buster is trying to convince Ophelia he can stay in a purely son/mother dynamic after the romantic rejection
jokes.punchline: His dramatic 'No, it's just my heart' (broken by rejection) is immediately undercut by '…Oh, it's my hand, too, actually' — the bionic arm has also broken. Buster's emotional declaration punctured by practical hardware failure.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best single moments. 'No, it's just my heart' is set up as a sincere dramatic line, and the immediate self-correction ('Oh, it's my hand, too, actually') destroys the pathos in the most Buster-specific way possible. The character integration is near-perfect — only someone with a broken bionic arm could deliver this joke. The structure is immaculate: sincere beat, pause, deflation.

jokes.text: Who is a better lover? / He is. / He is. Okay. / What about son?!
jokes.setup: Buster, rejected romantically, asks Ophelia to at least confirm his superiority in one category
jokes.punchline: 'What about son?!' — after accepting the romantic defeat, Buster's urgent follow-up is to confirm his son-ranking. This is where his priorities truly lie.
jokes.explanation: Possibly the episode's funniest moment in terms of pure character comedy. The entire romantic subplot resolves into Buster's real concern — being the better son. 'What about son?!' delivered with desperate urgency is perfectly calibrated. It crystallizes everything the episode has been about in three words. The setup (lover competition) misdirects us before the true punchline reveals Buster's actual priorities.

jokes.text: Buster: 'Who is a better lover?' / Ophelia: 'He is.' / Buster: 'He is. Okay.' / Buster: 'What about son?!'
jokes.setup: Buster is hurt by being replaced and asks Ophelia to compare
jokes.punchline: 'What about son?!' — after losing the lover competition, Buster immediately pivots to the son competition as a fallback, revealing that to him, son-hood is also a competitive category
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. The setup ('He is okay') is a quiet defeat; the pivot to 'What about son?!' is both desperately funny and perfectly characterizes Buster — he's prepared to compete on the son axis as a consolation prize. The delivery of 'He is. Okay.' followed by 'What about son?!' is exactly what distinguishes exceptional character comedy from ordinary jokes. Near-anchor level.

jokes.text: But not before using the wrong hand to punch a picture of Herbert. / Stupid Bust…!
jokes.setup: Buster is furious at Herbert and punches his photo
jokes.punchline: He uses the bionic arm — 'the wrong hand' — to punch a picture, presumably destroying both the picture and hurting himself significantly; he self-blames mid-word ('Stupid Bust…!')
jokes.explanation: The cut-off self-insult 'Stupid Bust…!' is a good comedic beat — we know the word he's starting and the interruption implies the physical consequence arrived before he could finish. Solid slapstick beat.

jokes.text: But I've got to find Lucille 2, I've got to give her pictures. / I don't know the songs. / It's prerecorded. / You're a monster, you don't have to be any good. / What? / Now just go Thing your heart out and don't be put off by the freakishly large…
jokes.setup: Tobias is enlisting Buster to appear in a musical as 'The Thing' from Fantastic Four
jokes.punchline: Tobias tells Buster to 'Thing your heart out' — using the character name as a verb — while assuring him that being a monster means he doesn't need to be any good. His description cuts off at 'freakishly large…' implying Buster's bionic arm completes the Thing costume.
jokes.explanation: 'Thing your heart out' is a lovely piece of Tobias-speak. The 'freakishly large' cut-off creates a satisfying visual implication. Tobias's confidence that quality doesn't matter for a monster is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Yes. / I've been with a black woman. / I was talking about your hand. / Oh. This huge thing? Yeah, it's new. / Of course, the week after I got it, the six came out. / You can play a flute with that one. / It comes with a flute!
jokes.setup: Lucille 2 notices Buster looks different; he interprets her observation differently than intended
jokes.punchline: Buster announces 'I've been with a black woman' when Lucille 2 meant to reference the bionic arm — then the arm discussion leads to a newer model coming out immediately after, one that 'comes with a flute'
jokes.explanation: The 'I've been with a black woman' non-sequitur before Lucille 2 clarifies is a great misdirection. But 'it comes with a flute!' is the unexpected escalation that truly lands — the bionic arm upgrade having a flute is absurdly specific and perfectly timed.

jokes.text: And he's gotten himself in some pretty hot ham water this time.
jokes.setup: Buster is explaining Herbert Love's prostitute scandal to Lucille 2
jokes.punchline: 'Hot ham water' instead of 'hot water' — a reference to the recurring Arrested Development gag where hot ham water is an extremely poor/cheap soup substitute. Buster has organically worked it into an idiom.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's great jokes. The substitution of 'hot ham water' for 'hot water' in an idiom works on multiple levels simultaneously: as a callback to the running gag, as a character-specific verbal quirk, and as a genuinely funny non-sequitur. The idiom modification is the kind of dense, rewarding wordplay that defines Arrested Development. Extremely quotable and rewatch-essential.

jokes.text: That's my wig.
jokes.setup: Buster shows Lucille 2 the photos of Herbert with a prostitute and Lucille 2 reacts
jokes.punchline: Her immediate identification of the wig worn by the prostitute — 'That's my wig' — suggests the prostitute is wearing her wig, which raises further disturbing questions about how it got there
jokes.explanation: Three-word punchline with enormous implied backstory. Lucille 2's wig being on the prostitute in Herbert's scandal photos is a perfect comedy bomb — the implication is left entirely to the audience's imagination, which makes it funnier. Highly quotable and perfectly economical.

jokes.text: Buster: 'That's my wig.' — reacting to seeing the prostitute in Herbert's photo is wearing a wig he apparently recognizes
jokes.setup: Buster shows Lucille 2 compromising photos of Herbert Love with a prostitute
jokes.punchline: 'That's my wig' — Buster recognizes his own wig on the prostitute in the photo, which raises multiple unanswered questions about how his wig got there
jokes.explanation: Three words that open an entire comedic mystery. 'That's my wig' is exceptional economy — we learn something shocking about Buster's life from his complete casual recognition. The joke is funnier for what it doesn't explain. Classic Arrested Development density.

jokes.text: Well, I hurt my mother, didn't I? I missed her trial, and she cut me out of her life. But I'm over that! / Really? / Then you have me to thank for that. / What do you mean? / Oh, Buster, you think missing her trial was an accident?
jokes.setup: Buster says he's grown by hurting his mother; Lucille 2 reveals she engineered the situation
jokes.punchline: Lucille 2 reveals she deliberately got Buster drunk on juice to make him miss the trial — a twist that reframes the entire episode's first act
jokes.explanation: A major structural reveal that retroactively makes the juice bender funnier — Lucille 2 engineered Buster's 'cran-apple rebellion' to sabotage Lucille Sr. The rewatch value is very high as the entire juice sequence takes on new meaning. The show's density at its best.

jokes.text: Although 'helped' might not be the best description of what she did. / In fact, she'd been laying in wait for him.
jokes.setup: Lucille 2 says she 'helped' Buster miss the trial
jokes.punchline: The narrator's dry correction — 'helped might not be the best description' — followed by 'laying in wait' reframes Lucille 2 as predatory, which is both funny and somewhat disturbing
jokes.explanation: Competent narrator correction. 'Laying in wait' is a good word choice. Works well as a transitional beat.

jokes.text: And once she'd gotten Buster into the apartment, she eschewed the all-natural juice for the kind with cartoon animals and riddles on the box. The type Buster had been known to have a weakness for.
jokes.setup: The narrator reveals how Lucille 2 got Buster drunk: by switching to juice boxes with cartoon animals
jokes.punchline: Buster's specific weakness — cartoon animal juice boxes with riddles — is both completely in character and the most elaborate possible seduction for an adult man. 'Drinking right out of the box' is the cherry on top.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character specificity. The idea that Buster has a documented 'weakness' for juice boxes with cartoon animals and riddles is both hilarious and deeply sad. 'Drinking right out of the box' as the sign of maximum debauchery is perfectly calibrated for Buster. Lucille 2 knowing this about him and exploiting it is darkly funny.

jokes.text: And soon, the partying was off the hook, with Buster drinking right out of the box. / Oh, this is just like Mother's Day Eve!
jokes.setup: Buster is on a juice box bender, and the narrator describes the 'party'
jokes.punchline: 'Off the hook' — the episode title and a pun on Buster's missing hand (the hook). Buster's exclamation 'This is just like Mother's Day Eve!' reveals that his idea of peak celebration is something that happens the night before Mother's Day.
jokes.explanation: Double-layered payoff — the episode title is itself a pun on Buster's hook hand, and the line 'off the hook' lands as both idiom and callback. 'This is just like Mother's Day Eve!' is an extraordinary character line — his peak party experience is the night before Mother's Day. Both jokes land simultaneously and reward rewatching.

jokes.text: I mean, how do you miss a sign like that? / Oh. / I was tinkling!
jokes.setup: In the flashback, Buster is having a moment of almost-clarity about the manipulation
jokes.punchline: 'I was tinkling!' — Buster's reason for missing the sign is that he was urinating (using childlike vocabulary). The near-insight is immediately deflated by the most mundane and infantile possible excuse.
jokes.explanation: 'Tinkling' is the perfect word — it's the most childlike possible vocabulary for urination, and it deflates the moment of potential adult insight perfectly. Character integration is near-perfect.

jokes.text: You kept me from mother? You sent her to prison? And this whole time, I thought about getting back together with you. / Well, you're a little late, honey. I've been dating your father for the past couple of weeks.
jokes.setup: Buster confronts Lucille 2 about manipulating him to miss his mother's trial
jokes.punchline: Lucille 2's response to his accusation is to casually mention she's now dating his father — which is simultaneously a deflection, a further betrayal, and a deeply Bluth family complication
jokes.explanation: Lucille 2 dating Buster's father as her response to being called out for manipulation is a perfect escalation that ties into the broader season arc. The casual delivery ('well, you're a little late, honey') makes it land as both funny and devastating.

jokes.text: Hey! I need to punch something! / Even if it is a poor little donkey. / But it wasn't that kind of donkey punch.
jokes.setup: Buster declares he needs to punch something; there are donkeys nearby (at Cinco)
jokes.punchline: 'But it wasn't that kind of donkey punch' — the narrator's aside references the vulgar term 'donkey punch' while immediately clarifying this is a different kind, in the most deadpan possible way
jokes.explanation: The narrator invoking and then immediately dismissing the vulgar meaning of 'donkey punch' is a bold, perfectly executed joke. The 'poor little donkey' setup and 'wasn't that kind' clarification is extremely well-timed. The narrator's deadpan complicity in naming the thing they're not describing is sophisticated comedy writing.

jokes.text: Oh, I need some juice. / Perhaps uno mas, señor, to feed to the fishes?
jokes.setup: Buster is at the Cinco event and needs juice; Gene Parmesan appears in disguise
jokes.punchline: Gene Parmesan's disguise-appropriate offer of 'uno mas señor to feed to the fishes?' is both a cover identity maintenance AND a darkly comic suggestion (feeding juice to fish). The 'uno mas' implies Buster has been drinking repeatedly.
jokes.explanation: Gene Parmesan's appearance is a nice callback for devoted viewers. His offering of juice 'to feed to the fishes' is a funny detail in an already-absurd scene.

jokes.text: Gene Parmesan. / This is not my real job.
jokes.setup: Buster recognizes Gene Parmesan in his festival disguise
jokes.punchline: 'This is not my real job' — Gene's clarification that his current role (apparently a festival vendor) is not his actual occupation is a perfect small moment of dignity-assertion from a recurring bit character
jokes.explanation: Gene's one-line dignity defense is a classic Arrested Development background-character moment. The economy is exceptional. Rewards viewers who know Gene Parmesan.

jokes.text: And so he blindsided him and became one.
jokes.setup: Buster confronts Herbert Love at Cinco and attacks him
jokes.punchline: 'Blindsided him and became one' — Buster physically blind-sides Herbert (hits him from the side unexpectedly) while simultaneously becoming the 'Blind Side Monster' nickname he'd been given. Double meaning delivered in one elegant sentence.
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect joke construction. 'He blindsided him and became one' does double duty as plot summary and wordplay payoff simultaneously. The sentence's elegance — its perfect structural completion of the running gag — is exactly what Arrested Development's narration does at its best. Both meanings are earned: physical action + thematic label. Among the episode's most carefully constructed jokes.

jokes.text: Oh, no. / I'm in the movie. / Oh, no.
jokes.setup: Buster has caused havoc at Cinco and sees himself on a security camera monitor
