jokes.punchline: 'I'm in the movie' — Buster doesn't realize he's on a security camera; he thinks he's being filmed for a movie. His dismay at being in 'the movie' has a meta quality given that Ron Howard (the narrator) is producing a movie about the Bluth family within the show's fiction.
jokes.explanation: The meta layer — Buster thinking security footage is a movie, while a movie about the Bluths is being made — is effectively layered. 'I'm in the movie' is both an error and potentially true. The double 'Oh, no.' bracketing the revelation is a nice comedic rhythm.

jokes.text: Buster, seeing himself on security camera footage of his rampage: 'Oh, no. I'm in the movie. Oh, no.'
jokes.setup: Buster has apparently just caused chaos at Cinco; he sees the security footage
jokes.punchline: 'I'm in the movie' — Buster's frame for the security camera footage is that he's accidentally appeared in a film, which reveals both his media literacy (or lack thereof) and his obliviousness to his own actions
jokes.explanation: 'I'm in the movie' is a great Buster line — his interpretation of security footage as cinema is perfectly consistent with his childlike worldview and his disconnect from consequence. Also meta-resonant given the documentary/movie framing of the show itself.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'Works on everything.' (about the Fakeblock privacy software) — immediately after Buster asks if it works on security cameras
jokes.setup: Buster needs to delete security footage and asks George Michael about Fakeblock
jokes.punchline: George Michael enthusiastically confirms Fakeblock works on everything — while we (the audience across Season 4) know Fakeblock is entirely fake software that doesn't actually work
jokes.explanation: The Fakeblock joke is maximally ironic for Season 4 viewers who know the software is fraudulent. George Michael's proud confidence about fake software that doesn't work being used for a genuine cover-up is darkly funny.

jokes.text: Have you got one with a case?
jokes.setup: George Michael hands Buster a hard drive with the Fakeblock software
jokes.punchline: Buster's first and only concern about the software that will supposedly save him from criminal prosecution is whether it comes with a protective case for the hard drive
jokes.explanation: Perfect character comedy moment — Buster's priorities are consistently and hilariously wrong. 'A case' for the hard drive as his primary concern in a crisis is exactly him. Maximum economy.

jokes.text: Buster goes into action and attempts to Fakeblock the footage. / Oh. / Sounds like it's working.
jokes.setup: Buster is attempting to use Fakeblock on the security system while Carl (the guard) steps away
jokes.punchline: 'Sounds like it's working' — Buster has no idea what Fakeblock does or how to use it, so his evidence that it's working is an audio cue, which means nothing. The irony is that Fakeblock does nothing.
jokes.explanation: The layered irony — Buster thinks sound = success, but Fakeblock doesn't work anyway — is effective for those following the season arc. Solid deadpan beat.

jokes.text: What she did to this boy.
jokes.setup: The episode ends with Buster having failed to cover his tracks, alone and in trouble
jokes.punchline: The narrator's final words — 'What she did to this boy' — are ambiguous: which woman? Lucille? Lucille 2? Ophelia? All of them? The phrase encompasses the entire episode's damage while also having a darkly comic resignation to it
jokes.explanation: A perfect final note — the narrator's five words encapsulate the entire episode's theme while maintaining plausible deniability about which woman they reference. The ambiguity is the point. Buster is still 'this boy' to the narrator. It's both a comedic deflation and a genuine thematic statement. The economy is exceptional.

jokes.text: Narrator final line: 'What she did to this boy.'
jokes.setup: The episode ends with Buster having tried to cover up apparent footage of himself doing something violent at Cinco
jokes.punchline: 'What she did to this boy' — the narrator's final editorial is deliberately ambiguous: which 'she' (Lucille? Lucille 2? Ophelia?) and what exactly was done to him implies both sympathy for Buster and accountability-shifting, in a voice that sounds like a disapproving relative
jokes.explanation: A superb closing line. The ambiguity of 'she' — pointing at multiple women who have shaped/damaged Buster — and the folksy 'what she did to this boy' framing (as if the narrator is a scandalized neighbor) is perfect tonal control. It's funny and genuinely unsettling simultaneously. Good economy, strong structure, high rewatch bonus as the ambiguity becomes more resonant across seasons.

tmdb_overview: Buster struggles to make it on his own and break away from his overbearing mother.
insight: Revised-season cringe finally clicks: 61 jokes land at franchise-high discomfort density.
